name: Bluebell Photography in the UK: A Practical Field Guide

step.name: Pack the night before, charge everything
step.text: Body, two lenses, polariser, tripod, kneeling mat, spare battery, spare card, lens cloth, weather cover, head torch. Charge everything. Coffee in a thermos.

step.name: Arrive in darkness, scout in low light
step.text: Be on site 30 minutes before sunrise. Walk the area in head-torch light, identifying densest patches and natural compositions. First 20 minutes is reconnaissance, next 60 is shooting.

step.name: Set custom white balance before you fire
step.text: Bluebells shift colour through the morning. Set custom WB off grey card or bare earth in the same light as subject. Re-set every hour. RAW shooters: use Cloudy or Shady, not AWB.

step.name: Work the wide carpet shots first
step.text: First light is the only window for atmospheric wide shots. Tripod low, 24-35mm, f/8-f/11, polariser. Look for a leading line. Bracket exposures for bright canopy gaps.

step.name: Switch to detail and backlit work as the sun rises
step.text: Once sun clears canopy, switch to 70-200mm or macro. Single stems backlit by sun for the glowing shots. Detail clusters at f/2.8-f/5.6, focus on front edge.

step.name: Leave by mid-morning, before walkers arrive
step.text: By 10am the light has hardened and walkers fill the woodland. Pack up. Stay on paths, leave nothing, never pick. Take any litter on the way out.

step.name: Review same day, edit lightly later
step.text: Cull on import, same day. 8-12 keepers from 200 frames typical. Edit the next evening, not the same night. Lift contrast, hold saturation. Soft purple-blue, not electric.
